_meta.description: Comprehensive contextual glosses for Isaiah and Malachi sections in the Book of Mormon. Each entry keys on book:chapter:verse and provides phrase-level annotations with short, factual, non-interpretive notes to help readers understand historical and cultural references.
_meta.format: Each gloss has: phrase (the exact text to underline), note (the tooltip text, max ~160 chars), and category (geographic, cultural, historical, literary)
_meta.coverage: 1 Nephi 20-21 (Isaiah 48-49), 2 Nephi 7-8 (Isaiah 50-52), 2 Nephi 12-24 (Isaiah 2-14), 2 Nephi 27 (Isaiah 29 expansion), 2 Nephi 30 (Isaiah 11 quotation), 3 Nephi 22 (Isaiah 54), 3 Nephi 24-25 (Malachi 3-4)

1nephi:20:1.note: The covenant people of Israel, Israel itself, or figuratively Jacob's descendants.

1nephi:20:1.phrase: out of the waters of Judah
1nephi:20:1.note: The waters flowing from Judah — likely the Jordan River or springs in Judah's territory.

1nephi:20:4.phrase: thy neck is an iron sinew, and thy brow brass
1nephi:20:4.note: A metaphor for strength — sinews of iron (muscles), forehead/brow of brass (unshakable will).

1nephi:20:9.phrase: for my name's sake will I defer mine anger
1nephi:20:9.note: God delays judgment to protect His own reputation and name in the world.

1nephi:20:10.note: Metal is refined in a furnace by heat — Israel's suffering will purify the nation like fire purifies ore.

1nephi:20:14.phrase: and his arm shall come upon the Chaldeans
1nephi:20:14.note: The Chaldeans were the dominant tribe in Babylon, making Babylon the superpower that conquered Judah in 586 BCE.
The 'him' likely refers to Cyrus, the Persian king who freed the Jews from Babylonian captivity (~550 BCE).

1nephi:21:5.phrase: and my God shall be my strength
1nephi:21:5.note: The servant relies on God's power, not on military or political might.

1nephi:21:6.note: The servant will bring God's word not just to Israel but to all the non-Israelite peoples of the world.

1nephi:21:7.note: The servant is scorned and rejected — a lowly, despised figure despite his cosmic importance.

1nephi:21:8.phrase: in an acceptable time have I heard thee
1nephi:21:8.note: God promises to answer the servant's prayer and help — 'acceptable time' means the right moment, when God wills.

1nephi:21:23.phrase: lick up the dust of thy feet
1nephi:21:23.note: A gesture of absolute submission and humility — enemies will grovel before Israel.

2nephi:7:1.phrase: Where is the bill of your mother's divorcement
2nephi:7:1.note: In ancient Israel, a man could divorce his wife by giving her a written bill — Isaiah says God never gave Judah such a document.

2nephi:7:4.note: A skilled speaker — literally, a tongue that knows how to speak well, to comfort the weary.

2nephi:7:6.phrase: my cheeks to them that plucked off the hair
2nephi:7:6.note: Extreme humiliation — having one's beard or cheek hairs violently torn out.

2nephi:7:7.phrase: For the Lord God will help me
2nephi:7:7.note: Despite the beating, the servant is confident in God's help.

2nephi:7:7.phrase: have I set my face like a flint
2nephi:7:7.note: The speaker does not turn away in disgrace or hide their face — they endure shame openly.

2nephi:7:8.phrase: And the Lord is near, and he justifieth me
2nephi:7:8.note: God, who vindicates the servant, is close — ready to act.

2nephi:8:1.phrase: to the hole of the pit from whence ye are digged
2nephi:8:1.note: Sarah — literally, the pit or womb from which Israel was born. A poetic parallel to the rock (Abraham).

2nephi:8:3.note: A promise that desolate land will flourish like the Garden of Eden — fertility will be restored.

2nephi:8:24.note: Uncircumcision was a mark of being outside God's covenant; 'unclean' meant spiritually defiled or removed from the temple.

2nephi:12:1.note: Not the prophet Amos; Amoz was Isaiah's father, likely of a noble Judahite family.

2nephi:12:2.phrase: the mountain of the Lord's house
2nephi:12:2.note: Mount Moriah in Jerusalem, site of Solomon's temple.

2nephi:12:2.phrase: in the top of the mountains
2nephi:12:2.note: Isaiah pictures the temple mount raised above all peaks — the highest point on earth.

2nephi:12:3.phrase: house of the God of Jacob
out of Zion shall go forth the law
2nephi:12:3.note: Zion and Jerusalem are used as parallel terms for the same holy city.

2nephi:12:4.note: Farm tools forged from weapons — a reversal of Joel 3:10, which says the opposite.
Curved blades used to trim grapevines — a common tool in Israelite agriculture.

2nephi:12:5.note: The covenant people of Israel, descended from the patriarch Jacob.

2nephi:12:6.note: Filled with pagan practices from Mesopotamia — Babylon, Assyria, and surrounding cultures.
Fortune-tellers. The Philistines, Israel's coastal neighbors, were known for divination.

2nephi:12:6.phrase: please themselves in the children of strangers
2nephi:12:6.note: Forming alliances (or intermarrying) with foreign nations instead of trusting God.

2nephi:12:7.note: Horses and chariots were offensive military technology — and a sign of reliance on human power rather than God (see Deut. 17:16).

2nephi:12:9.note: "Mean" here means common or ordinary — the everyday person, not someone cruel.

2nephi:12:12.phrase: the day of the Lord of Hosts
2nephi:12:12.note: A recurring Old Testament concept: a future time when God acts decisively to judge the proud and deliver the humble.

2nephi:12:13.note: The tallest, most prized trees in the ancient world — used to build palaces and temples. A symbol of towering pride.
Bashan (modern Golan Heights) was famous for its massive oak forests. Second only to Lebanon's cedars as a symbol of strength.

2nephi:12:15.note: A fortified wall — the military defenses cities relied on for survival.

2nephi:12:16.note: Large merchant vessels for long-distance trade. Tarshish was likely in Spain or Sardinia — the edge of the known world.
Probably luxury goods or fine craftsmanship — the Hebrew suggests beautiful display vessels or carved imagery.

2nephi:13:1.phrase: the stay and the staff, the whole staff of bread, and the whole stay of water
2nephi:13:1.note: Everything a society leans on — its food, its water, its leaders. Isaiah now lists every support God is removing.

2nephi:13:2.note: Isaiah names every pillar of society — military, civic, religious, skilled trades — to show that God is removing all of them.

2nephi:13:3.note: A skilled craftsman — metalworker, builder, or engineer. "Cunning" here means expert, not deceitful.

2nephi:13:4.phrase: children unto them to be their princes
2nephi:13:4.note: Inexperienced, unqualified rulers — the opposite of the wise leaders just listed. A sign of total social collapse.

2nephi:13:5.note: Social order inverted — the lowly disrespect those of rank. Isaiah is describing anarchy.

2nephi:13:6.phrase: Thou hast clothing, be thou our ruler
2nephi:13:6.note: Things are so desperate that simply owning a coat qualifies someone to lead — everyone else has been stripped bare.

2nephi:13:9.phrase: their sin to be even as Sodom
2nephi:13:9.note: Sodom (Genesis 18-19) was the ultimate example of a city destroyed for its wickedness. The comparison means Judah's sin is open and shameless.

2nephi:13:12.phrase: children are their oppressors, and women rule over them
2nephi:13:12.note: Not a statement about women or children in general — Isaiah is saying the expected leaders have failed, and the social order is upside down.

2nephi:13:14.phrase: ye have eaten up the vineyard
2nephi:13:14.note: The vineyard is Israel (see Isaiah 5:1-7). The leaders have devoured what they were supposed to tend.

2nephi:13:15.phrase: grind the faces of the poor
2nephi:13:15.note: A vivid image of economic oppression — crushing people like grain between millstones.

2nephi:13:16.note: The wealthy women of Jerusalem. Isaiah uses their luxury as a symbol of the whole city's misplaced priorities.
A haughty posture — head tilted back, looking down at others.
Flirtatious glances. The Hebrew suggests eyes painted or decorated to attract attention.
Taking small, affected steps — possibly because of ankle chains that restricted their stride (see v. 18).

2nephi:13:16.phrase: making a tinkling with their feet
2nephi:13:16.note: Ankle bracelets with bells or ornamental chains that jingled with each step.

2nephi:13:17.phrase: smite with a scab the crown of the head
2nephi:13:17.note: A skin disease on the most visible part of the body — public humiliation replacing public vanity.

2nephi:13:18.note: Ankle bracelets, probably with small bells or chains.
Ornamental hairnets or headbands, often beaded.
Crescent-shaped pendants worn as necklaces — "tires" means headdresses or ornaments, not wheels.

2nephi:13:19.note: Veils or long scarves covering the face and neck.

2nephi:13:20.note: Decorative ankle chains connecting the feet — these also caused the "mincing" walk of v. 16.
Perfume boxes or scent bottles — the Hebrew literally means "houses of the soul" (i.e., containers for fragrance).

2nephi:13:22.note: Festival robes — fine garments kept for special occasions.
Outer cloaks or shawls draped over the body.
Despite the name, probably money-bags or ornamental purses — not hair pins.

2nephi:13:23.note: Polished metal mirrors — not eyeglasses, which wouldn't exist for another two thousand years.

2nephi:13:24.phrase: instead of a girdle, a rent
2nephi:13:24.note: Instead of a fine sash, a rope. "Rent" here means a torn strip of cloth.
A richly embroidered chest garment — replaced by rough sackcloth, the garment of mourning.
A brand-mark (from captivity or slavery) replacing their carefully maintained appearance.

2nephi:13:26.phrase: her gates shall lament and mourn
2nephi:13:26.note: City gates were the center of public life — where courts met, business happened, and news spread. Empty gates meant a dead city.

2nephi:13:26.phrase: she shall be desolate, and shall sit upon the ground
2nephi:13:26.note: Sitting on the ground was the posture of mourning and humiliation (see Lamentations 1:1).

2nephi:14:1.phrase: seven women shall take hold of one man
2nephi:14:1.note: So many men have died in war (13:25) that seven women share one husband. The number seven suggests completeness — the devastation is total.

2nephi:14:1.phrase: we will eat our own bread, and wear our own apparel
2nephi:14:1.note: Normally a husband provided food and clothing. These women waive that right — they only want his name to avoid the social shame of being unmarried and childless.
In ancient Israel, being unmarried and childless was considered a deep disgrace for a woman.

2nephi:14:2.note: A sprouting shoot from a seemingly dead stump — new life after devastation. A recurring prophetic image (see also Isa. 11:1, Jer. 23:5, Zech. 3:8).

2nephi:14:2.phrase: them that are escaped of Israel
2nephi:14:2.note: The surviving remnant — those who endure the judgment described in chapters 12-13.

2nephi:14:3.phrase: written among the living in Jerusalem
2nephi:14:3.note: An ancient concept of a divine registry — a book of those counted as belonging to God's people (see Exod. 32:32, Ps. 69:28).

2nephi:14:4.phrase: the spirit of judgment and by the spirit of burning
2nephi:14:4.note: Purification through judgment — like refining metal with fire to burn away impurities.

2nephi:14:5.phrase: a cloud and smoke by day and the shining of a flaming fire by night
2nephi:14:5.note: Echoes the Exodus (Exod. 13:21-22), when God guided Israel with a pillar of cloud by day and fire by night. The protection returns.

2nephi:14:5.phrase: upon all the glory of Zion shall be a defence
2nephi:14:5.note: The Hebrew word is chuppah — a canopy or wedding covering. God shelters the renewed Zion like a bride.

2nephi:14:6.note: A shelter or booth — echoing both the wilderness tabernacle and the harvest booths of the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot).

2nephi:15:1.phrase: to my well-beloved a song of my beloved
2nephi:15:1.note: A term of deep affection — indicates the speaker has strong emotional investment in what follows.
In Isaiah, the vineyard represents Israel — God's planted, tended possession.
A choice location — the best possible conditions for growing grapes. God gave Israel every advantage.

2nephi:15:2.phrase: and also made a wine-press therein
2nephi:15:2.note: Stone basins for crushing grapes and fermenting wine — expensive to build, essential for the vintage.

2nephi:15:2.phrase: and it brought forth wild grapes
2nephi:15:2.note: The vineyard failed completely — produced the opposite of what was expected. Wild grapes were useless.

2nephi:15:4.phrase: what could have been done more to my vineyard
2nephi:15:4.note: A rhetorical question — God did everything right; the failure is entirely the vineyard's (Israel's).

2nephi:15:5.phrase: I will take away the hedge thereof
2nephi:15:5.note: God will remove the protection He provided — Israel will face its enemies without shelter.

2nephi:15:5.phrase: and it shall be eaten up
2nephi:15:5.note: Enemies will overrun the land — a military invasion and occupation.

2nephi:15:6.note: Complete destruction — the land will become desolate, abandoned.

2nephi:15:6.phrase: it shall not be pruned nor digged
2nephi:15:6.note: No one will tend the vineyard — agriculture and civilization cease.

2nephi:15:8.note: The wealthy are expanding their estates by buying or seizing neighboring properties — consolidating power.

2nephi:15:8.phrase: that they may be placed alone in the midst of the earth
2nephi:15:8.note: A few elites end up monopolizing the land, leaving the common people displaced.

2nephi:15:10.phrase: Yea, ten acres of vineyard shall yield one bath
2nephi:15:10.note: A Hebrew unit of liquid measure, roughly 5-6 gallons. Used for wine and oil.
A Hebrew unit of dry measure, roughly 0.7 bushels. Used for grain and flour.
A larger unit of measure — 10 ephahs or 10 baths. A 'homer' of grain was a large load.

2nephi:15:11.phrase: Wo unto them that rise up early in the morning
2nephi:15:11.note: "Wo" (woe) is Isaiah's cry of judgment — six 'wo' pronouncements follow, each condemning a specific vice.
Pursuing drunkenness from early morning — a sign of dissipation and moral collapse.

2nephi:15:13.phrase: my people are gone into captivity
2nephi:15:13.note: Isaiah predicts exile — the ultimate judgment for covenant-breaking.
A lack of wisdom and understanding of God's will — not ignorance but willful refusal to listen.

2nephi:15:18.phrase: Wo unto them that draw iniquity with cords of vanity
2nephi:15:18.note: A vivid metaphor: sin dragged along like something on a rope — inevitable, inexorable.

2nephi:15:20.phrase: Wo unto them that call evil good, and good evil
2nephi:15:20.note: Complete moral inversion — calling wickedness virtue and virtue wickedness.

2nephi:15:26.note: A standard or banner raised to call attention — foreign armies will come at God's signal.
A sharp, piercing sound to summon people — like whistling for a dog. A gesture of contempt and dominion.

2nephi:16:1.phrase: in the year that king Uzziah died
2nephi:16:1.note: Uzziah (Azariah) of Judah ruled c. 783-742 BCE — his death marks a moment of national vulnerability.

2nephi:16:1.phrase: I saw also the Lord sitting upon a throne, high and lifted up
2nephi:16:1.note: Isaiah's famous throne-room vision — he sees the divine court and receives his commission.

2nephi:16:2.note: Fiery, angelic beings (literally 'burning ones') with six wings each — guardians of God's holiness.
The seraphim cover their faces — even celestial beings dare not look directly at God.
A gesture of respect and submission — covering the lower body, the sign of reverence.

2nephi:16:3.phrase: Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord of Hosts
2nephi:16:3.note: The threefold repetition emphasizes God's absolute holiness — purity beyond all measure.

2nephi:16:3.phrase: the whole earth is full of his glory
2nephi:16:3.note: God's presence and majesty fill the entire cosmos — there is nowhere God is absent.

2nephi:16:4.phrase: the posts of the door moved
2nephi:16:4.note: The temple's doorposts shake — a physical sign of God's awesome power and presence.

2nephi:16:4.phrase: the house was filled with smoke
2nephi:16:4.note: A cloud of divine presence — often called 'shekinah' in Jewish tradition. The glory of God made visible.

2nephi:16:5.phrase: Wo is unto me! for I am undone
2nephi:16:5.note: Isaiah cries out in terror — he is unclean and standing before perfect holiness. He expects to be destroyed.
In temple language, unclean meant disqualified from approaching God. Isaiah confesses his unworthiness.

2nephi:16:6.note: A burning piece of coal from the altar — taken by a seraph with tongs.

2nephi:16:7.phrase: he laid it upon my mouth, and said: Lo, this has touched thy lips
2nephi:16:7.note: The burning coal purifies Isaiah's speech — he is cleansed and commissioned to speak for God.

2nephi:16:8.phrase: Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?
2nephi:16:8.note: God asks for a volunteer — God does not force unwilling servants into service.
Isaiah's eager response — completely willing, despite the cost he will face.

2nephi:16:10.note: Blind their eyes, deafen their ears — God hardens the people so they cannot respond to His message.

2nephi:16:11.phrase: until the cities be wasted without inhabitant
2nephi:16:11.note: The judgment continues until Israel is emptied — cities in ruins, houses abandoned, land desolate.

2nephi:16:13.phrase: as a teil tree, and as an oak
2nephi:16:13.note: The teil or terebinth tree — a tall, sturdy tree. Even it will be stripped of leaves and life.

2nephi:17:3.phrase: the conduit of the upper pool in the highway of the fuller's field
2nephi:17:3.note: A specific location in Jerusalem where cloth-makers worked — where Ahaz and Isaiah meet.

2nephi:17:14.phrase: and shall call his name Immanuel
2nephi:17:14.note: A name meaning 'God is with us' — a sign that God has not abandoned Judah despite the invasion.

2nephi:17:18.phrase: the bee that is in the land of Assyria
2nephi:17:18.note: Bees swarm in Assyria — similarly, a metaphor for the Assyrian army that will devastate Judah.

2nephi:17:20.note: Hiring a barber's razor — Assyria is God's tool, hired to shave (devastate) Judah.

2nephi:18:3.note: Isaiah's second son — name means 'swift is the booty, speedy is the prey.' A sign that invasion is imminent.

2nephi:18:6.phrase: the waters of Shiloah that go softly
2nephi:18:6.note: The gentle spring water that feeds Jerusalem via the Gihon tunnel — a symbol of Jerusalem's quiet security.

2nephi:18:16.note: Seal up the teaching — preserve Isaiah's words despite current rejection, for a future generation.

2nephi:19:1.phrase: by the way of the Red Sea beyond Jordan in Galilee of the nations
2nephi:19:1.note: Northern Israel and Galilee — territories that will suffer most under Assyrian conquest (722 BCE).

2nephi:19:2.phrase: The people that walked in darkness have seen a great light
2nephi:19:2.note: After exile and oppression comes the Messiah — light breaking into the darkest moment.

2nephi:19:6.phrase: Wonderful, Counselor, The Mighty God, The Everlasting Father, The Prince of Peace
2nephi:19:6.note: Five names or titles for the promised child — each describes an aspect of His character and role.

2nephi:19:6.phrase: unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given
2nephi:19:6.note: A royal birth announcement — the promised ruler who will bring peace and justice.

2nephi:19:10.phrase: the sycamores are cut down, but we will change them into cedars
2nephi:19:10.note: Boasting that destruction can be easily repaired — arrogant confidence in human recovery.

2nephi:19:11.phrase: Therefore the Lord shall set up the adversaries of Rezin
2nephi:19:11.note: God will raise up enemies (Assyria) against Syria (Rezin) — enemies of Judah's foes.

2nephi:19:12.phrase: his hand is stretched out still
2nephi:19:12.note: A refrain repeated in Isaiah 5, 9, and 10 — God's judgment continues until complete repentance.

2nephi:20:5.note: Assyria is God's tool or instrument — used to punish Israel without being aware it is serving God's purposes.

2nephi:20:9.note: Assyria boasts of conquering one city after another — each name is a fallen capital, proving no one can resist.

2nephi:20:15.phrase: Shall the ax boast itself against him that heweth therewith?
2nephi:20:15.note: Rhetorical: an ax cannot boast or claim credit — it is merely God's tool, used by His hand.

2nephi:20:28.note: Isaiah traces the Assyrian army's march town by town toward Jerusalem — each name brings the threat closer.

2nephi:20:32.note: The final location — right at Jerusalem's gates. The invasion has reached the capital.

2nephi:20:33.phrase: Behold, the Lord, the Lord of Hosts shall lop the bough with terror
2nephi:20:33.note: God intervenes — the Assyrian advance is cut off, not by human effort but divine action.

2nephi:20:33.phrase: and the haughty shall be humbled
2nephi:20:33.note: The arrogant Assyrian king will be brought low — judgment for his boasting.

2nephi:21:1.phrase: a rod out of the stem of Jesse
2nephi:21:1.note: Jesse was King David's father — a new Davidic king will sprout from David's family line.

2nephi:21:1.phrase: a Branch shall grow out of his roots
2nephi:21:1.note: A shoot from Jesse's stump — new life after apparent death. David's dynasty continues.

2nephi:21:2.phrase: the spirit of the Lord shall rest upon him
2nephi:21:2.note: The promised ruler is empowered by a sevenfold spirit — wisdom, understanding, counsel, might, knowledge, and reverence for God.

2nephi:21:6.phrase: The wolf also shall dwell with the lamb
2nephi:21:6.note: Natural predators and prey will live in peace — the entire created order is transformed.

2nephi:21:8.phrase: And the sucking child shall play on the hole of the asp
2nephi:21:8.note: A venomous serpent and a legendary serpent/dragon — even the most dangerous creatures are harmless.

2nephi:21:9.phrase: the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the Lord
2nephi:21:9.note: Universal knowledge of God — everyone will recognize and understand God's character and will.

2nephi:21:11.phrase: the Lord shall set his hand again the second time
2nephi:21:11.note: A second exodus — God will gather scattered Israel from distant lands, just as He once rescued them from Egypt.

2nephi:21:12.phrase: and he shall set up an ensign for the nations
2nephi:21:12.note: Non-Israelite peoples will turn to this ruler — His dominion is universal.

2nephi:21:13.phrase: The envy of Ephraim also shall depart
2nephi:21:13.note: The northern kingdom (Ephraim) will no longer be jealous of or opposed to the south (Judah).

2nephi:21:13.phrase: and the adversaries of Judah shall be cut off
2nephi:21:13.note: Judah's enemies will be eliminated — the southern kingdom's troubles will end.

2nephi:21:14.phrase: But they shall fly upon the shoulders of the Philistines towards the west
2nephi:21:14.note: Israel will expand westward against the Philistines — territorial expansion under the promised ruler.

2nephi:21:15.phrase: and shall smite it in the seven streams
2nephi:21:15.note: The sea is divided into smaller channels — no longer an impassable barrier to escape or return.

2nephi:22:1.phrase: And in that day thou shalt say
2nephi:22:1.note: A hymn of thanksgiving — the exiles sing praise when they are restored.

2nephi:23:1.note: 'Burden' (masaa) is Isaiah's term for a prophetic judgment-oracle against a nation.

2nephi:23:2.note: A signal to assemble armies — God is summoning forces against Babylon.
Shout and call — the summons to war is urgent and loud.

2nephi:23:3.note: God's consecrated warriors — summoned to execute His judgment against Babylon.

2nephi:23:8.phrase: pangs and sorrows shall take hold of them
2nephi:23:8.note: Labor pains — the terror of judgment described as the agony of childbirth.

2nephi:23:12.phrase: I will make a man more precious than fine gold; even a man than the golden wedge of Ophir
2nephi:23:12.note: Ophir was a legendary source of gold (possibly in Arabia or East Africa) — gold as the height of wealth.

2nephi:23:13.note: Cosmic upheaval — God's judgment destabilizes the entire created order, not just one nation.

2nephi:24:19.note: The king's offspring will be despised and cut off — no dynasty will continue.

2nephi:24:23.phrase: I will also make it a possession for the bittern
2nephi:24:23.note: A marsh bird — Babylon will be swampy, desolate wasteland instead of a mighty city.

2nephi:24:23.phrase: and I will sweep it with the besom of destruction
2nephi:24:23.note: A broom that sweeps clean — God will sweep Babylon away completely.

2nephi:24:29.phrase: for out of the serpent's root shall come forth a cockatrice
2nephi:24:29.note: Mythical deadly creatures — representations of judgment and danger from God.

3nephi:22:1.phrase: for more are the children of the desolate than the children of the married wife
3nephi:22:1.note: The barren woman represents exiled, abandoned Israel — her children will outnumber those at home.

3nephi:22:2.phrase: Enlarge the place of thy tent
3nephi:22:2.note: Expand the dwelling — Israel will need more space for the multitude returning from exile.
Extend the tent ropes — a tent metaphor for Israel's expansion and growth.

3nephi:22:4.phrase: forget the shame of thy youth
3nephi:22:4.note: Erase the memory of past humiliation and exile — Israel's widowhood and disgrace are over.

3nephi:22:8.phrase: in a little wrath I hid my face from thee for a moment
3nephi:22:8.note: God's judgment was brief and limited — He did not abandon Israel permanently.

3nephi:22:8.phrase: everlasting kindness will I have mercy on thee
3nephi:22:8.note: God's mercy will outlast His wrath — restoration is permanent and eternal.

3nephi:22:11.phrase: behold, I will lay thy stones with fair colors
3nephi:22:11.note: Precious, colorful stones — Jerusalem will be rebuilt with beauty and magnificence.

3nephi:22:12.note: Striped or banded stones — another precious material for the new city.
Red gemstones, possibly rubies — jewels symbolizing the wealth and beauty of restored Zion.

3nephi:22:12.phrase: all thy borders of pleasant stones
3nephi:22:12.note: Blue precious stones — used for Jerusalem's ornamental rebuilding.

3nephi:22:15.phrase: Behold, they shall surely gather together against thee
3nephi:22:15.note: Enemies' attempts to harm Israel will fail — God protects the restored people.

3nephi:24:1.phrase: Behold, I will send my messenger
3nephi:24:1.note: Malachi ('my messenger') announces a forerunner who will prepare the way for God.

3nephi:24:1.phrase: and he shall prepare the way before me
3nephi:24:1.note: The messenger clears obstacles — prepares the people for God's arrival.

3nephi:24:2.phrase: For he is like a refiner's fire, and like fuller's soap
3nephi:24:2.note: Extreme heat and harsh cleansing — God's refining will be drastic, not gentle.

3nephi:24:3.phrase: he shall sit as a refiner and purifier of silver
3nephi:24:3.note: God personally supervises the purification — watches closely to ensure quality.
The priestly tribe will be purified — temple worship will be cleansed and restored.

3nephi:24:4.phrase: the offering of Judah and Jerusalem
3nephi:24:4.note: Offerings made by a purified, righteous people — worship with integrity, not hollow ritual.

3nephi:24:5.phrase: come near to you to judgment
3nephi:24:5.note: God comes as judge — not to save but to condemn those who practice injustice.

3nephi:24:8.note: A shocking accusation — Israel has withheld tithes and offerings that belong to God.

3nephi:24:10.note: Sacrificial meat for temple feasts — sustenance for the priesthood and the sanctuary.

3nephi:24:11.note: Likely locusts or other pests that consume crops — Israel's agricultural prosperity is threatened.

3nephi:24:11.phrase: and he shall not destroy the fruits of your ground
3nephi:24:11.note: Crops and produce — the basic sustenance Israel depends on for survival.

3nephi:24:16.note: A divine record of those who fear God — echoes the concept of a registry of the righteous.

3nephi:24:17.phrase: they shall be mine, saith the Lord of hosts, in that day when I make up my jewels
3nephi:24:17.note: The faithful will be God's prized possession — treasures more valuable than earthly wealth.

3nephi:25:1.note: Extreme heat and destruction — the day of judgment will be hot and consuming.

3nephi:25:2.phrase: But unto you that fear my name, shall the Son of Righteousness arise with healing in his wings
3nephi:25:2.note: A metaphor for the Messiah or God's justice — arising like the sun to bring light.
The wings of a bird carry healing — a tender image of comfort and restoration.

3nephi:25:3.phrase: ye shall tread down the wicked
3nephi:25:3.note: The righteous will walk over the ashes of the destroyed wicked — total victory.

3nephi:25:4.phrase: Remember ye the law of Moses
3nephi:25:4.note: A final charge: keep the Torah. The covenant given at Sinai remains binding.

3nephi:25:5.phrase: I will send you Elijah the prophet
3nephi:25:5.note: The great prophet (c. 850 BCE) expected to return before God's final judgment — a herald of restoration.

3nephi:20:36.phrase: put on thy beautiful garments, O Jerusalem, the holy city
3nephi:20:36.note: Quoted from Isaiah 52:1. Jerusalem is called to dress as for a celebration — the mourning is over.
Those outside God's covenant. Circumcision was the physical sign of Israel's covenant with God.

3nephi:20:37.note: Jerusalem personified as a woman in chains — a common prophetic image for a conquered city.

3nephi:20:40.phrase: How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him
3nephi:20:40.note: Quoted from Isaiah 52:7. In the ancient world, messengers ran between cities on foot. Beautiful feet = welcome news.

3nephi:20:41.phrase: be ye clean that bear the vessels of the Lord
3nephi:20:41.note: From Isaiah 52:11. The Levites who carried the sacred temple vessels had to be ritually pure.

3nephi:20:42.phrase: the God of Israel shall be your rearward
3nephi:20:42.note: From Isaiah 52:12. 'Rearward' means rear guard — God protects both the front and back of the march.

3nephi:20:44.phrase: his visage was so marred, more than any man
3nephi:20:44.note: From Isaiah 52:14. 'Visage' means face or appearance — disfigured beyond recognition.

3nephi:20:45.phrase: So shall he sprinkle many nations
3nephi:20:45.note: From Isaiah 52:15. Sprinkling was a priestly act of ritual purification (see Lev. 14:7, Num. 19:18).

3nephi:21:14.phrase: I will cut off thy horses out of the midst of thee
3nephi:21:14.note: From Micah 5:10. Horses and chariots represent military self-reliance rather than trust in God.

3nephi:21:16.note: From Micah 5:12. Sorcery and divination — seeking guidance from sources other than God.
Fortune-tellers or diviners. A practice Israel adopted from surrounding nations.

3nephi:21:17.note: Carved idols — statues of gods made from wood or stone, then overlaid with metal.
Sacred stone pillars (Hebrew: matsevot) set up at Canaanite worship sites.

3nephi:21:18.note: Not natural forests — ritual poles or trees (Hebrew: asherah) associated with Canaanite goddess worship.

1nephi:21:2.phrase: in his quiver hath he hid me
1nephi:21:2.note: The container holding arrows — God hides the servant there, ready to deploy him when needed.

2nephi:15:9.phrase: In mine ears, said the Lord of Hosts
2nephi:15:9.note: God directly speaks to Isaiah — a private, intimate revelation.

2nephi:21:7.phrase: And the cow and the bear shall feed
2nephi:21:7.note: Even cattle and dangerous predators share pasture peacefully.

2nephi:21:7.phrase: their young ones shall lie down together
2nephi:21:7.note: The next generation inherits peace — the transformation is permanent, not temporary.

2nephi:23:6.phrase: as a destruction from the Almighty
2nephi:23:6.note: Shaddai, God as all-powerful and terrifying — judgment as overwhelming devastation.

2nephi:23:19.note: Destroyed cities of the plain (Genesis 19) — the ultimate examples of divine destruction.

2nephi:23:21.note: Mournful, wailing animals — possibly hyenas or jackals, symbols of desolation.
Wild creatures of the night and ruins — the Hebrew (seirim) may mean hairy demons or simply desert animals.

2nephi:24:13.phrase: in the sides of the north
2nephi:24:13.note: North was the direction of the gods' dwelling in ancient mythology — the king claims divinity.

2nephi:17:15.phrase: Butter and honey shall he eat
2nephi:17:15.note: Simple, basic foods — a sign of either pastoral simplicity or of a land ravaged and depopulated.

2nephi:19:21.note: The two northern tribes (offspring of Joseph) — they will turn against each other in civil war.

2nephi:22:3.phrase: draw water out of the wells of salvation
2nephi:22:3.note: God is a source of deliverance and help — like wells providing water in the desert.

2nephi:8:22.note: A metaphor for God's wrath — Jerusalem drank from this cup of judgment during the Babylonian conquest.

2nephi:18:14.phrase: a stone of stumbling, and for a rock of offense
2nephi:18:14.note: God (or the faith Isaiah proclaims) becomes an obstacle to those who reject Him — they stumble over it.

2nephi:23:22.note: Monstrous serpent-creatures — Babylon will be a place of danger and wildness.

2nephi:24:14.phrase: I will ascend above the heights of the clouds
2nephi:24:14.note: The king of Babylon claims a divine throne — blasphemous ambition to sit among the gods on the cosmic mountain.

1nephi:11:16.note: In the 1828 Webster's dictionary: 'a voluntary descent from rank, dignity, or just claims' — not the modern sense of being patronizing.

1nephi:11:26.note: In the 1828 Webster's dictionary: 'a voluntary descent from rank, dignity, or just claims' — not the modern sense of being patronizing.

jacob:5:35.note: In early English, 'evil' often meant simply 'bad' or 'worthless' — here it describes inedible fruit, not moral wickedness.

2nephi:27:4.note: From Isaiah 29:9. 'Stay yourselves' means stop and stand still — God dares them to pause and see what is really happening.

2nephi:27:27.phrase: your turning of things upside down shall be esteemed as the potter's clay
2nephi:27:27.note: The schemers taunt God: 'Your efforts to overturn our plans? We'll reshape them like clay.' They claim to be the potter and treat God as the clay — a breathtaking inversion that God immediately rebukes.

2nephi:27:28.phrase: Lebanon shall be turned into a fruitful field
2nephi:27:28.note: From Isaiah 29:17. Lebanon was famous for its vast cedar forests. God will turn wild woodland into cultivated farmland — a total reversal of the natural order.

2nephi:27:32.phrase: lay a snare for him that reproveth in the gate
2nephi:27:32.note: The city gate was the public court where disputes were settled. To 'reprove in the gate' is to speak up for justice in open court. These people are punished for it.

2nephi:30:9.phrase: he shall smite the earth with the rod of his mouth
2nephi:30:9.note: From Isaiah 11:4. The 'rod of his mouth' is his word — he judges and rules by what he speaks, not by force of arms.

2nephi:30:11.phrase: righteousness shall be the girdle of his loins, and faithfulness the girdle of his reins
2nephi:30:11.note: From Isaiah 11:5. In Hebrew, the loins and 'reins' (kidneys) represented a person's deepest core. Isaiah pictures the Messiah wearing righteousness and faithfulness like a belt — these qualities aren't accessories but what holds him together.

2nephi:30:14.phrase: the sucking child shall play on the hole of the asp
2nephi:30:14.note: From Isaiah 11:8. The asp and cockatrice (viper) are deadly serpents. Even the most dangerous creatures are harmless — a vision of total peace.

alma:5:27.note: In biblical Hebrew, 'walking' (halakh) is the root metaphor for manner of life and ethical conduct — the root of 'halakha' (Jewish law). Alma asks: have you conducted your lives blamelessly?

alma:11:3.phrase: the judge received for his wages
alma:11:3.note: The following verses describe a binary-based currency system. Gold: senine (1), seon (2), shum (4), limnah (7). Silver: senum (1, equal to a senine of gold), amnor (2), ezrom (4), onti (7). A judge earned one senine of gold per day. Any amount can be made from a combination of these units — similar to how modern coins work.

alma:1:1.phrase: the first year of the reign of the judges
alma:1:1.note: The Nephite calendar counts from when Alma the Younger became the first chief judge, replacing the monarchy (~91 BC). ‘The fifth year of the judges’ = ~87 BC. This dating system runs through Alma, Helaman, and into 3 Nephi.

helaman:1:1.phrase: the fortieth year of the reign of the judges
helaman:1:1.note: The Nephite calendar counts from when judges replaced kings (~91 BC). The fortieth year = ~52 BC. About 50 years before Christ’s birth.

3nephi:1:1.phrase: the ninety and first year had passed away
3nephi:1:1.note: Two calendars overlap here: the reign of the judges (91st year = ~1 BC) and 600 years from Lehi’s departure from Jerusalem (~600 BC). Both point to the same moment — the eve of Christ’s birth.

alma:11:22.note: An onti is the largest silver unit, worth 7 senums. Six onties = 42 senums = 42 days’ wages for a judge. Zeezrom is offering roughly six weeks’ salary — a substantial bribe designed to tempt Amulek publicly.
